steps.reveal: Quis = who? / which man? (nom., interrogative pronoun)
habens = having / who has (pres. act. participle of 'habere')

hints.gerund: A gerund would be 'habendi/habendo' — 'habens' is a participle modifying 'Quis'
hints.gerundive: A gerundive would be 'habendus' — 'habens' is a present active participle
hints.perfect participle: Perfect participle would be 'habitus' (passive) — 'habens' is present active

hints.habito, habitare, habitavi, habitatum: These are the parts of 'habitare' (to dwell)
hints.abeo, abire, abii, abitum: These are the parts of 'abire' (to go away)
hints.capio, capere, cepi, captum: These are the parts of 'capere' (to take)

steps.reveal: centum oves = a hundred sheep (acc., object of 'habens')

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would also be 'oves,' but here it is the object of the participle 'habens'
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'ovibus' — 'oves' is accusative object of 'habens'
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'ovium' — 'oves' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'ovis' (gen.) ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'ovis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'ovis' ends in -is

hints.to endure: 'To endure' is 'perferre' or 'pati' — 'perdere' means to lose
hints.to complete: 'To complete' is 'perficere' — 'perdere' means to lose or destroy

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'perdidit' — 'perdiderit' with -erit is subjunctive
hints.future perfect indicative: Future perfect looks identical in form — context (conditional question) indicates subjunctive
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'perdidisset' — 'perdiderit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the parts of 'dare' (to give) — 'perdere' is a compound of 'dare' but with different conjugation pattern
hints.pendo, pendere, pependi, pensum: These are the parts of 'pendere' (to weigh/pay)
hints.perficio, perficere, perfeci, perfectum: These are the parts of 'perficere' (to complete)

steps.reveal: unam = one (acc. f. sg., object of 'perdiderit')

hints.nominative: Nominative feminine would be 'una' — 'unam' with -am is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative feminine would be 'una' — 'unam' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive feminine would be 'unius' — 'unam' is accusative

steps.prompt: What type of question does this passage introduce?
steps.target: a rhetorical question expecting an obvious answer
choices: a rhetorical question expecting an obvious answer

hints.a genuine information question: Jesus is not seeking information — the answer is obvious and leads into the parable
hints.an indirect question: An indirect question would be subordinate — this is a main clause with 'Quis'
hints.a deliberative question: Deliberative questions ask 'what should I do?' — this asks 'who among you would not...'

steps.prompt: This verse opens which famous parable?
steps.target: the Parable of the Lost Sheep
choices: the Parable of the Lost Sheep
the Parable of the Prodigal Son
the Parable of the Good Samaritan

hints.the Parable of the Prodigal Son: The Prodigal Son follows later in Luke 15 — this opening about sheep is the Lost Sheep
hints.the Parable of the Good Samaritan: The Good Samaritan is in Luke 10 — this is about a man losing one of a hundred sheep
hints.the Parable of the Sower: The Sower is in Luke 8 — this parable concerns a lost sheep from a flock of 100

translation: Which man among you, having a hundred sheep and having lost one of them
